summary: Jeff Atwood explores the tension between user customization and software usability, drawing on insights from a Microsoft Vista UI designer. The post uses the Honda Civic aftermarket modification analogy to explain why deep OS skinning isn't officially supported - it creates support nightmares and can accidentally harm usability. While acknowledging that power users should have customization options, Atwood argues that most of the 700 million Windows users are fine with defaults. The core tension is that user-created skins are often worse than professionally designed defaults, making broad customization a double-edged sword.
key_points: Deep UI customization is far more invasive than it appears on the surface, affecting supportability and usability
The Honda Civic analogy: manufacturers allow some customization, but extensive modifications void your warranty for good reason
Making customization broadly accessible to casual users risks making things worse when they accidentally install poor skins
Professional UI designers create defaults with usability expertise that random skin creators typically lack
The vast majority of users are well-served by thoughtful defaults rather than unlimited customization
money_quotes: But it is far more invasive than it may appear to be on the surface.
Once you make it a broad reaching thing that anyone can download and install – maybe even accidentally – you run the risk of really making things worse for people.
Depending on how much you do under the hood, you might void your warranty.
The default UI may not have flames/aliens/J-Lo on it, but it was probably built by designers versed in usability.
Customization is in the eyes of the beholder, of course, but the odds of a user picking a random skin that actually reduces usability is high.
key_insight: Unlimited customization sounds like freedom but often degrades the user experience, because professional defaults embody expertise that casual customizers lack.
